toolUseResult.prompt:
You are implementing task F7 — the jnxweb Python 3.6 web GUI client — for a market-data feed-handler project in the git repo /workspace/jnx-feed. Do NOT run any git commands (the orchestrator commits). Phases F0–F6 are committed and green; do not modify existing files except where stated.

CONTEXT:
- A C++ feed handler (cpp/build/jnxfh) multicasts one UPDATE record per exchange message (except timestamps) to UDP group 239.192.1.1:26400 (dev: loopback iface, IP_MULTICAST_LOOP). Each ~433-byte record carries FULL per-ticker state: static (isin, round_lot, limits...), state (trading_state, short_sell, ref_price...), top-10 aggregated book levels + totals, trade summary (last trade + cum qty/turnover/count), envelope (epoch, pub_seq, exch_seq, exch_ns, trigger char, ticker, group), and an order-level delta section clients ignore. Because every record is full state, a missed datagram self-heals on the next update for that ticker — clients need loss STATS only, no recovery.
- jnxweb/records.py already exists (committed): py3.6-safe decode/encode for all record kinds. USE IT — do not duplicate parsing. Read it first for the exact API and field names. docs/wire_spec.md is the layout contract.
- Existing consumer to imitate for multicast socket setup: tools/mcast_spy.py (group join via IP_ADD_MEMBERSHIP, SO_REUSEADDR etc.).
- Full local pipeline for live testing: 
    cpp/build/jnxdb --sock=/tmp/f7.sock --query_port=27701 &
    python3 -m jnxfeed.sim --itch-file tests/fixtures/sample_udp.itch --itch-port 25831 --glimpse-port 25832 [--speed ...? check --help; use a paced mode for a long-running demo, max speed for quick tests] &
    cpp/build/jnxfh --itch_host=127.0.0.1 --itch_port=25831 --user=TEST --pass=SECRET --db_sock=/tmp/f7.sock --bootstrap=replay --mcast_group=239.192.1.1 --mcast_port=26400 --mcast_if=127.0.0.1 &

HARD ENVIRONMENT RULES (violations = task failure):
- jnxweb/ is PRODUCTION code for Python 3.6 on RHEL 8: stdlib only, and it must BOTH run on the dev interpreter (python3 = 3.14) AND pass `python3 tools/py36check.py <files>`. Read /workspace/jnx-feed/JNX_PLAN.md §0 for the full 3.6 rules: NO asyncio (banned entirely), no dataclasses, no walrus, no f-string`=`, no capture_output/text kwargs, selectors-based single-threaded I/O like the prototype (see jnxfeed/net/reactor.py for the established pattern).
- No pip, no external packages, no CDN assets — the web page must be fully self-contained (inline CSS/JS).

FIRST read: /workspace/jnx-feed/JNX_PLAN2.md Phase F7 (all subsections — implementation steps, boundary conditions, verification, pitfalls) and §1 architecture; jnxweb/records.py; tools/mcast_spy.py; JNX_PLAN.md §0.

DELIVERABLES (per plan F7, restated):
1. jnxweb/mcast.py — non-blocking multicast receiver registered on a shared selectors loop; also a --test-feed injection path: an optional AF_UNIX datagram socket path accepted as an alternative record source (lets tests inject canned records without multicast; document it).
2. jnxweb/state.py — ticker -> latest decoded UPDATE dict; per-ticker recent-trades ring (last 50, appended only when trigger=='E'); global stats (updates, pub_seq gap count, bad datagrams, last epoch); ON EPOCH CHANGE: clear all state, remember event for a UI banner ("feed restarted").
3. jnxweb/httpd.py — hand-rolled HTTP server on the SAME selectors loop (single thread, no http.server, no threading): GET / -> the page; GET /tickers -> JSON sorted ticker list; GET /snap/<ticker> -> JSON full state incl. trades ring (404 JSON if unknown); GET /stats -> JSON global stats; GET /ws -> WebSocket upgrade. Careful HTTP basics: Content-Length, minimal request parsing (method+path+headers), 404/400 paths, connection close handling.
4. jnxweb/wsock.py — minimal RFC 6455 SERVER: handshake Sec-WebSocket-Accept = base64(sha1(key + "258EAFA5-E914-47DA-95CA-C5AB0DC85B11")) — unit-test against the RFC 6455 §1.3 example key "dGhlIHNhbXBsZSBub25jZQ==" -> "s3pPLMBiTxaQ9kYGzzhZRbK+xOo="; server->client TEXT frames UNmasked; parse client frames (which ARE masked): text (subscription JSON {"sub":"TICKER"}), close (reply close), ping (reply pong); fragmentation unsupported (reject/close, documented). Per-client: one subscribed ticker at a time; push that ticker's full JSON on updates, coalesced to max 10 pushes/s per client (latest wins, via loop timer); send-buffer cap 256 KB -> drop client with WARN, never block the loop.
5. jnxweb/static_page.py — one embedded HTML+JS+CSS page: ticker input + clickable list (from /tickers, refreshed periodically), static/state panel, 10-level book table (bid qty/price | price/ask qty, best row on top), last-50 trades table (newest first), stats footer (updates/s, gaps, ws connection state), auto-reconnecting WebSocket, "feed restarted" banner on epoch change. Prices are raw ints with 1 implied decimal — render /10 with one decimal in JS (0x7FFFFFFF = show "-"). Keep it clean and readable; no external fonts/libs; fine to be minimal-pretty (monospace tables ok).
6. jnxweb/__main__.py — args: --mcast 239.192.1.1:26400, --http-port 8080, --mcast-if 127.0.0.1, --test-feed <uds-path> (optional). Wire everything on one selectors loop. INFO log lines per plan format. Clean SIGINT shutdown.
7. tools/ws_probe.py — dev/ops helper (also 3.6-safe): raw-socket WebSocket CLIENT for verification: connects, handshakes (client frames masked per RFC!), sends {"sub": "<ticker>"}, prints N frames (--frames N), exits 0. No external libs.
8. tests/unit/test_jnxweb.py (+ more files if cleaner) — pytest, no live multicast needed: wsock handshake RFC vector; frame encode/decode incl. masked client frames, 16-bit and 64-bit lengths, ping/pong, close; state epoch-clear and trades-ring; httpd routing via a real socketpair or loopback socket against the running loop in-process (spin the selectors loop in the test thread or step it manually — keep it deterministic, no sleeps >0.2s); end-to-end-ish: start jnxweb as a subprocess with --test-feed, inject canned UPDATE records built with jnxweb.records encoders, then assert /tickers, /snap/<ticker>, /stats via urllib.request, and a ws_probe subscription receives frames with correct fields. Collision-safe ports.

BOUNDARY CONDITIONS (from plan): unknown ticker snap -> 404 {"error":"unknown"}; ws sub to unknown ticker -> {"error":"unknown"} then data flows if it appears later; epoch change mid-session -> clear + banner; slow client -> capped + dropped without stalling; multiple concurrent browsers on different tickers; datagram with bad magic/version -> count bad, ignore; pub_seq gaps -> stats only.

VERIFICATION (run all, paste outputs):
  python3 tools/py36check.py jnxweb/*.py tools/ws_probe.py     # ALL clean
  python3 -m pytest tests/unit/test_jnxweb.py -q
  python3 -m pytest -q                                          # whole suite green
  LIVE demo (paste): start the full pipeline (commands above, paced sim), then:
    python3 -m jnxweb --http-port 18080 --mcast-if 127.0.0.1 &
    curl -s localhost:18080/tickers            # non-empty JSON list while replaying
    curl -s localhost:18080/snap/<a-ticker-from-the-list> | head -c 400
    curl -s localhost:18080/stats
    python3 tools/ws_probe.py localhost:18080 <ticker> --frames 5   # 5 live frames printed
  Clean up all processes. Also: after the sim finishes at max speed, /stats gap count on loopback should be small/zero — report it.

PITFALLS (plan F7): asyncio/websockets/aiohttp DO NOT EXIST here — hand-roll on selectors; browser->server frames are masked, server->browser NOT; unbounded JSON buffering to a slow client is the classic bug — cap it; json with sort_keys=True for stable tests; SO_REUSEADDR before bind. Final message: files created, verification outputs incl. live-demo transcript and the gap count, design notes (coalescing, test-feed), any deviations.

content.text:
F7 is dispatched on a fresh Sonnet agent. It's building the operator-facing web client entirely in Python 3.6 stdlib: the multicast receiver (plus a Unix-socket test-feed injection path so tests don't depend on multicast), the per-ticker state cache with epoch-change reset, a hand-rolled HTTP server and RFC 6455 WebSocket server on a single `selectors` loop, the embedded single-page GUI (10-level book, last-50 trades, auto-reconnecting WebSocket, "feed restarted" banner), and a `ws_probe.py` client for headless verification.

The prompt pins the classic traps: no asyncio (banned on 3.6 anyway), masked client frames vs unmasked server frames with the RFC handshake test vector, per-client send-buffer caps so a slow browser can't stall the loop, and 10 Hz coalescing per subscription. Verification includes a live run against the full sim → jnxfh → multicast pipeline with curl and ws_probe transcripts, plus `py36check` clean on every production file. I'll verify (including my own live pipeline run) and commit when it lands.

<result>## Summary

Task F7 (jnxweb Python 3.6 web GUI client) is implemented and verified.

**Files created:**
- `/workspace/jnx-feed/jnxweb/mcast.py` — non-blocking multicast/UDS receiver on the shared `selectors` reactor; `--test-feed` path (`open_test_feed_socket`) documented in the module docstring
- `/workspace/jnx-feed/jnxweb/state.py` — ticker→latest-UPDATE cache, 50-entry trade ring (trigger `'E'` only), global stats, epoch-change clear + restart callback
- `/workspace/jnx-feed/jnxweb/httpd.py` — hand-rolled HTTP server (no `http.server`, no threads) with `/`, `/tickers`, `/snap/<ticker>`, `/stats`, `/ws` routes
- `/workspace/jnx-feed/jnxweb/wsock.py` — RFC 6455 server: handshake, frame codec (16/64-bit lengths, masked client / unmasked server), `WebSocketClient` (ping/pong, close, fragmentation-reject, 256KB cap, 10/s coalesced pushes) and `WsHub`
- `/workspace/jnx-feed/jnxweb/static_page.py` — self-contained HTML/CSS/JS operator page
- `/workspace/jnx-feed/jnxweb/__main__.py` — CLI wiring, INFO/WARN logging in the §0.3 format, SIGINT shutdown
- `/workspace/jnx-feed/tools/ws_probe.py` — raw-socket WebSocket client probe (masked client frames)
- `/workspace/jnx-feed/tests/unit/test_jnxweb.py` (29 tests) and `test_jnxweb_e2e.py` (4 tests, subprocess + `--test-feed`)

**Verification:**
- `python3 tools/py36check.py jnxweb/*.py tools/ws_probe.py` → clean
- `python3 -m pytest tests/unit/test_jnxweb.py tests/unit/test_jnxweb_e2e.py -q` → 33 passed
- `python3 -m pytest -q` → 272 passed (whole suite)
- Live demo: full pipeline (`jnxdb` + paced `jnxfeed.sim --speed 3000` + `jnxfh` + `jnxweb --http-port 18080`) — `/tickers` populated live, `/snap/<ticker>` returned full state+trades, `/stats` reported clean counters, `tools/ws_probe.py` printed 5 live frames with increasing `pub_seq`/`exch_seq`. After the sim finished: **`gaps: 0`, `bad: 0`**, `updates: 215017` vs jnxfh's `published: 216346` (99.4% received — the ~0.6% is trailing-tail UDP loss from a burst that briefly outpaced Python's single-threaded decode rate; since no further packet arrives afterward to reveal it, it's inherently invisible to pub_seq-based gap detection, same limitation `tools/mcast_spy.py` has). All processes cleaned up afterward.

**Design notes / deviations:**
- Coalescing is exact latest-wins: `WebSocketClient.queue_update` overwrites `_pending_payload` and schedules at most one `reactor.call_later` timer per client for the 100ms window.
- `--test-feed` binds an `AF_UNIX SOCK_DGRAM` socket; a peer injects complete wire records with `sendto(bytes, path)` — used by the e2e test and documented in `jnxweb/mcast.py`'s docstring.
- `HttpServer`/`__main__.py` support binding `--http-port 0` and expose the actual bound port via `server.port` plus a `jnxweb listening on HOST:PORT` stdout line for collision-safe test ports.
- All JSON responses use `sort_keys=True` for deterministic test assertions, per the plan's pitfall note.
- No deviations from the plan's boundary conditions: unknown-ticker snap → 404 `{"error":"unknown"}`; ws sub to unknown ticker → `{"error":"unknown"}` immediately, then data flows once it appears; epoch change clears state and broadcasts `{"event":"restarted"}`; oversized per-client send buffer drops the client with a WARN log, never blocking the loop; bad-magic/short datagrams are counted and ignored.

result: F7 (jnxweb Python 3.6 web GUI client) is fully implemented — mcast.py, state.py, httpd.py, wsock.py, static_page.py, __main__.py, tools/ws_probe.py, and tests/unit/test_jnxweb*.py all pass py36check and pytest (272/272 suite-wide), and a live demo against the full C++ pipeline showed working /tickers, /snap, /stats, and 5 live WebSocket frames with a final gap count of 0.</result>

sleep 1

input.prompt:
You are implementing task F8 — the final phase: system hardening, benchmark, ops docs, target validation kit — for a market-data feed-handler project in the git repo /workspace/jnx-feed. Do NOT run any git commands (the orchestrator commits). Phases F0–F7 are committed and green.

CONTEXT (all committed and working):
- C++ (cpp/, plain GNU make, flags -std=c++11 -Wall -Wextra -Werror -pedantic -O2 -g -pthread, gcc 15 dev / gcc 8.5 RHEL 8.10 target, ZERO external deps): binaries jnxdb (in-memory DB: --sock= --query_port=), jnxfh (feed handler: --itch_host/--itch_port/--user/--pass/--db_sock/--bootstrap=replay|glimpse/--mcast_group/--mcast_port/--mcast_if [--glimpse_host/--glimpse_port]), tools itch_replay/book_dump/gen_record_vectors. `make -C cpp test` = 65 tests across 7 binaries; `make -C cpp all` builds everything.
- Python: prototype package jnxfeed/ (incl. simulator `python3 -m jnxfeed.sim --itch-file F --itch-port N --glimpse-port M [--speed X] [--drop-after] [--glimpse-cut]`), production client jnxweb/ (python 3.6 target; `python3 -m jnxweb --http-port P --mcast-if 127.0.0.1`), tools/ (dbquery.py, mcast_spy.py, compare_db_dump.py, run_e2e.py with scenarios baseline/kill_fh/kill_db/drop_exchange/glimpse_cold/kill_both, py36check.py, proto_state_dump.py, ws_probe.py). Whole pytest suite: 272 passed. Docs: JNX_PLAN2.md (the plan — read Phase F8 and §8), docs/wire_spec.md, RECOVERY.md, CONNECTIVITY.md.
- Fixtures: tests/fixtures/sample_udp.itch (222189 msgs; golden counts: decode total=222189, published updates=216346), sample_udp_head.itch (2000).
- Known perf points: python prototype ~379k msg/s decode+apply; C++ book_dump full day ~0.34 s; full pipeline (sim → jnxfh → jnxdb+mcast) previously ran the day in ~2.5 s wall incl. sim overhead.
- Dev env: Ubuntu, gcc 15, python3=3.14, NO docker, valgrind availability unknown (check; if absent use sanitizers).

FIRST read /workspace/jnx-feed/JNX_PLAN2.md Phase F8 (objective/steps/boundary/verification/pitfalls) and §8 (target-machine checklist).

DELIVERABLES:
1. Benchmark — `make -C cpp bench` target + a small driver (script or C++ tool as fits): reports a table of (a) decode-only rate (itch_replay over the full fixture, timed), (b) decode+apply rate (book_dump timed), (c) full-pipeline rate: sim at max speed → jnxfh → jnxdb + multicast, measured as published-updates/sec from jnxfh start to session end (parse jnxfh/jnxdb stats; exclude process startup where practical). Each best-of-3. Append the results table to README.md (mark it as dev-container numbers, alongside the existing Python prototype figure). Acceptance floor: pipeline ≥ 500k msg/s — if the sim itself is the bottleneck (likely: it's Python), ALSO measure and report a sim-less pipeline number (e.g. a small C++ feeder tool or jnxfh reading... simplest: report jnxfh's own processing rate over its live loop from its stats líne, and/or add a replay-file mode ONLY if trivial — do not over-engineer; document methodology honestly).
2. Sanitizer gate — Makefile target `test-asan`: rebuilds tests+modules into cpp/build-asan/ with -fsanitize=address,undefined (own flags var, no -Werror interaction issues, keep -O1 -g), runs all test binaries. Fix ANY findings in the C++ (minimal fixes, all existing tests must stay green). Also run the head-fixture pipeline (jnxdb+jnxfh asan builds vs sim) under ASAN and fix leaks — note: one-shot tools may "leak" at exit by design; suppress only with justification. If valgrind exists, a bonus `test-valgrind` is welcome but ASAN is the gate.
3. Soak check — extend tools/run_e2e.py with `--scenario soak --minutes M`: loops the head fixture through the sim (restart sim each pass, FH reconnects/resyncs — this doubles as repeated-recovery exercise; or use sim loop mode if it has one), polling RSS of jnxdb+jnxfh (from /proc) every 15 s; PASS if post-warmup (first 20%) RSS growth < 5% for both processes. Run at least 10 minutes and report the curve summary. jnxdb/jnxfh: if you find unbounded growth, fix it (bounded rings, no per-message allocation leaks).
4. OPERATIONS.md — start order (any; components tolerate absence — cite RECOVERY.md), sample config files under etc/ (jnxdb.cfg, jnxfh.cfg, jnxweb flags documented; the C++ binaries accept cfg file + --key=value via jnx::Cfg — check cpp/common/cfg.h and main.cpp usage for the file argument convention, add one if missing... check first, likely --config=path or first positional; if none exists, add --config= support consistently to both mains, minimal change), monitoring cheatsheet (the 5 s stats lines with field meanings, dbquery STATS fields, mcast_spy usage), failure playbook pointer to RECOVERY.md, dry-run instructions (simulator-based, exact commands).
5. Dist + target validation — Makefile target `dist` (top-level Makefile or cpp/Makefile, your call): builds dist/jnx-fh2-<date>.tar.gz containing cpp/ sources+Makefile, jnxweb/, tools/{dbquery,mcast_spy,ws_probe,py36check}.py, etc/ configs, docs (wire_spec, OPERATIONS, RECOVERY, TARGET_VALIDATION), tests/fixtures/sample_udp_head.itch; MUST build with `make -C cpp all` on a box with only gcc-c++ and make (no git/python needed for the C++ build — verify by extracting to a temp dir and building there). TARGET_VALIDATION.md: the plan §8 checklist as copy-paste commands for the RHEL 8.10 box (gcc/python version checks, tarball build+test, py36check+jnxweb --help on real 3.6, loopback dry run with expected numbers, multicast reachability test between hosts, then UAT probes per CONNECTIVITY.md).
6. Housekeeping: ensure `make -C cpp test` still green, whole pytest suite still green, py36check still clean on jnxweb/* and the 3.6-targeted tools.

VERIFICATION (run all, paste outputs):
  make -C cpp bench                     # table printed; note the numbers
  make -C cpp test-asan                 # zero ASAN/UBSAN reports across all tests
  python3 tools/run_e2e.py --scenario soak --minutes 10        # PASS, RSS summary
  make dist && ls dist/ && (extract to /tmp, make -C cpp all there, run cpp/build/itch_replay on the bundled head fixture -> total=2000 ... errors=0)
  make -C cpp test 2>&1 | tail -2 && python3 -m pytest -q | tail -1
  python3 tools/py36check.py jnxweb/*.py tools/dbquery.py tools/mcast_spy.py tools/ws_probe.py

PITFALLS (plan F8): ASAN clean BEFORE chasing benchmarks; separate build dirs (build-asan/ must not pollute build/); bench excludes sim pacing; RSS plateau judged after warmup, orders peak intraday — use fixture-loop steady state; -O2 stays for bench builds. Final message: files created/changed, the bench table, ASAN findings fixed (if any), soak RSS summary, dist verification transcript, deviations.

content.text:
F7 is now committed (`3c97289` — the earlier attempt was blocked by a transient harness issue, retry succeeded), and **F8 — the final phase — is running on a fresh Sonnet agent**. Its scope: the `make bench` throughput table (decode / decode+apply / full pipeline, with honest methodology notes since the Python simulator will likely be the pipeline bottleneck), an ASAN/UBSAN gate over all tests plus a live pipeline run (fixing anything it finds), a 10-minute RSS soak via `run_e2e.py` that doubles as a repeated-recovery exercise, `OPERATIONS.md` + sample configs + a `dist` tarball proven to build standalone with only gcc and make, and `TARGET_VALIDATION.md` — the copy-paste checklist for the real RHEL 8.10 box.

When it completes I'll re-verify, commit F8, and give you the full Phase 2 wrap-up. That will close out every phase of JNX_PLAN2.md that can be done without the target machine or the real exchange line.

<summary>Agent "F8 hardening, bench, ops docs" finished</summary>
<note>A task-notification fires each time this agent stops with no live background children of its own. The user can send it another message and resume it, so the same task-id may notify more than once.</note>
<result>Everything builds and tests cleanly from a fresh state. Final summary of the work.

**Files created:**
- `cpp/common/procstat.h` — `/proc/self/status` VmRSS reader (used by jnxdb/jnxfh stats lines and the soak check)
- `tools/bench.py` — F8 benchmark driver (`make -C cpp bench`)
- `tools/run_asan_pipeline.py` — ASAN/UBSAN pipeline gate helper (`make -C cpp test-asan`)
- `tools/make_dist.py` — builds `dist/jnx-fh2-<date>.tar.gz` (`make dist` / `make -C cpp dist`)
- `OPERATIONS.md`, `TARGET_VALIDATION.md`
- `etc/jnxdb.cfg`, `etc/jnxfh.cfg`

**Files modified:**
- `cpp/Makefile` — added `test-asan`, `test-valgrind` (no-ops without valgrind), `bench`, `dist` targets, own ASAN flags/build tree (`build-asan/`)
- `cpp/db/jnxdb_main.cpp`, `cpp/db/query.cpp` — `rss_kb` in STATS log/query line; renamed `--cfg=` to `--config=` for consistency with jnxfh
- `cpp/fh/jnxfh_main.cpp` — `rss_kb` in the 5s stats line
- `tools/run_e2e.py` — added `soak` scenario (`build_looped_fixture`, `rss_kb`, `run_soak`), added `rss_kb` to `EXCLUDED_STATS_FIELDS`
- `Makefile`, `README.md`, `.gitignore` (added `build-asan/`)

**Benchmark table** (best-of-3, dev container): decode-only ~980–995k msg/s, decode+apply ~605–655k msg/s, full pipeline (sim→jnxfh→jnxdb+mcast) ~36–40k msg/s — below the 500k floor because the Python simulator, not jnxfh/jnxdb, is the bottleneck (documented honestly in README.md and `tools/bench.py`'s output, with decode+apply as the honest sim-less ceiling).

**ASAN/UBSAN:** zero findings — all 65 unit tests plus the head-fixture sim→jnxfh→jnxdb pipeline run clean under `-fsanitize=address,undefined`, no fixes needed.

**Soak (10 min, ran for real):** PASS — jnxdb RSS +0.00%, jnxfh RSS +0.35% after warmup, ~172k messages processed continuously via one looped-fixture session, no restarts, no fixes needed.

**Dist verification:** `make dist` → `dist/jnx-fh2-20260712.tar.gz` (0.15 MiB), extracted to a scratch dir, `make -C cpp all` built clean with zero non-toolchain dependencies, `itch_replay` on the bundled `sample_udp_head.itch` printed `total=2000 ... errors=0`; `py36check.py` and `python3 -m jnxweb --help` both work from the extracted tree (using the minimal `jnxfeed/net/reactor.py` shim it also ships).

**Housekeeping:** `make -C cpp test` (65 tests/7 binaries) green, `python3 -m pytest -q` (272 tests) green, `py36check.py` clean on jnxweb/* and the target tools.

**Deviations:** soak scenario restarts neither jnxdb nor jnxfh mid-run (deviating from the plan's literal "restart-both" phrasing) — instead it feeds one continuous session from a pre-concatenated looped fixture, which is the only way to get a genuine multi-hour-equivalent leak signal without RSS resetting on every restart; this is explained in `tools/run_e2e.py` and `OPERATIONS.md` §5. `etc/jnxweb.cfg` was not created since jnxweb has no file-based config (flags only, documented in OPERATIONS.md instead).

result: F8 (system hardening, benchmark, ops docs, target validation) is complete — `make -C cpp bench`/`test-asan`/`dist` all pass, a real 10-minute soak run showed flat RSS (PASS), the dist tarball builds and runs cleanly in isolation, and both `make -C cpp test` (65/65) and `python3 -m pytest -q` (272/272) stay green.</result>

content.text:
**Phase 2 is complete — all nine phases (F0–F8) of JNX_PLAN2.md are implemented, verified, and committed** (`2fe23a5` plan → `834a3b2` F8, one commit per phase as requested).

**What exists now**, all zero-dependency and target-compatible:
- **`jnxdb`** (C++11) — single-threaded in-memory DB: 5 tables keyed by ticker, UDS ingest with the HELLO/GET_STATE/RESET/SYNC recovery protocol, TCP query interface.
- **`jnxfh`** (C++11) — SoupBinTCP client with reconnect-resume and GLIMPSE bootstrap, the parity-proven market core, one atomic 433-byte UPDATE per exchange message to the DB and to multicast; restarts recover from the DB and resume at `last_seq+1`.
- **`jnxweb`** (Python 3.6 stdlib) — multicast subscriber with a hand-rolled HTTP + WebSocket server and a live per-ticker book/trades page.
- Ops kit: `OPERATIONS.md`, `RECOVERY.md`, `TARGET_VALIDATION.md`, sample configs, and a `make dist` tarball verified to build standalone with only gcc + make.

**F8 verification (re-run by me):** ASAN/UBSAN zero findings across all 65 C++ tests and a live pipeline run; 272 Python tests green; 10-minute soak with flat RSS (jnxdb +0.00%, jnxfh +0.35%); dist tarball extracted cold and built/ran clean. Bench: decode ~990k msg/s, decode+apply ~630k msg/s; the full-pipeline figure (~38k msg/s) is capped by the *Python simulator*, not the C++ — the honest sim-less ceiling is the 630k decode+apply number, and this is documented plainly in the README.

**Worth your attention:**
1. **`jnxweb` imports `jnxfeed.net.reactor`** (the prototype's event loop) rather than being fully self-contained — the dist tarball ships a shim so production deploys work, but if you'd prefer jnxweb strictly standalone, that's a small refactor task.
2. The F6 recovery testing **found and fixed a real bug** (resync could emit wrongly-keyed rows for tickers whose orders had all closed) — the kill-scenario gates earned their keep.
3. Remaining items need things this environment doesn't have: the `TARGET_VALIDATION.md` checklist on a real RHEL 8.10 box (gcc 8.5 compile, real Python 3.6 run), and eventually the UAT probes from CONNECTIVITY.md when Japannext access arrives.

Agent usage: five Sonnet subagents total, reused across tasks where context helped (F0→F1, F3→F4) and started fresh when transcripts got heavy (F6, F7, F8), never more than two concurrent — with every phase independently re-verified and committed from this session.

As a final wrap-up, write up an overall "2 pages" doc explaining the full exercise (both phase1 and 2, with main focus on 2). The Phase 1 (python only) was confirmed to be perfect. Give also a bullet-points executive summary.

Also, write down a proposal doc for a Phase 3 - main target would be real production deployment, so hardening even more the architecture and improving monitoring/support capability would be key. Another goal would be generalizing the architecture so that some shared component can be reused for multiple exchanges / protocols. (including FIX / multicasts / etc)

And last, output the full conversation log to a file. Include the subagent conversations as well, as separated files.

input.content:
# Japannext ITCH Feed Handler — Project Wrap-Up (Phases 1 & 2)

*2026-07-12 — repo `/workspace/jnx-feed`, plans `JNX_PLAN.md` (Phase 1) and `JNX_PLAN2.md` (Phase 2)*

## Executive summary

- **Goal**: evaluate AI-agent-driven development on a realistic market-data project — a Japannext PTS equities ITCH feed handler — built entirely from public specs, with no access to a real exchange line.
- **Phase 1 (Python prototype)**: delivered in full and **confirmed perfect in real use** — SoupBinTCP connectivity, complete ITCH v2.02 decoding, GLIMPSE snapshot recovery, order books/trades, CLI views, plus an offline exchange simulator. It connected to the exchange, recovered messages, and displayed live market data correctly.
- **Phase 2 (production-shaped C++ system)**: a three-process architecture — C++ feed handler (`jnxfh`), C++ in-memory database (`jnxdb`), Python 3.6 web GUI (`jnxweb`) — implemented in 9 gated phases (F0–F8), one commit per phase, **all complete**.
- **Correctness anchor**: the C++ market core is proven **bit-identical** to the trusted Python prototype over the full 222,189-message official sample — a diff of canonical state dumps that stayed empty through every later change.
- **Resilience proven live**: five kill/restart scenarios (FH crash, DB crash, exchange drop, snapshot cold-start, dual crash) all converge to state identical to an uninterrupted run; the testing itself flushed out and fixed one real recovery bug.
- **Performance**: C++ decode ~990k msg/s, decode+apply ~630k msg/s (vs 379k for the Python prototype's decode+apply); the full 222k-message trading day replays through the entire pipeline in seconds; RSS flat over a 10-minute soak; ASAN/UBSAN clean.
- **Constraints honored throughout**: C++11 only, Python 3.6 stdlib only, zero third-party dependencies (target: locked-down RHEL 8.10), all enforced mechanically (strict compiler flags, an AST-based py3.6 linter) because the dev container runs much newer toolchains.
- **Process**: all coding by Sonnet worker agents (≤2 concurrent) driven by detailed per-phase task prompts from the plan; every phase independently re-verified by the orchestrator before commit. Test count grew from 203 (Phase 1) to 65 C++ + 272 Python tests.
- **Remaining before go-live**: run `TARGET_VALIDATION.md` on a real RHEL 8.10 box, then UAT connectivity per `CONNECTIVITY.md` when Japannext access is granted.

## Phase 1 in brief (the foundation Phase 2 stood on)

Phase 1 (plan `JNX_PLAN.md`, tasks T1.1–T7.2) built a pure-Python 3.6, stdlib-only prototype: SoupBinTCP client with lossless reconnect-resume, declarative ITCH codec, GLIMPSE snapshot bootstrap, reference data / order-book / trade-tape stores, CLI views, and — critically — an **exchange simulator** and **golden fixtures** extracted from Japannext's official sample pcaps (222,189 messages, byte-verified against the spec). It was validated against the real exchange and worked as designed. Three Phase-1 artifacts became Phase 2's safety net: the proven data-handling logic (the porting reference), the simulator (the stand-in test line), and the golden sample (the regression oracle).

## Phase 2: what was built

**Architecture** — one exchange message becomes exactly one atomic unit of work end to end:

```
exchange/simulator ──SoupBinTCP──▶ jnxfh (C++) ──UDS records──▶ jnxdb (C++, 5 tables)
                                      │
                                      └──UDP multicast (full-state records)──▶ jnxweb (Py3.6) ──▶ browser
```

- **`jnxfh`** owns the live market state (the ported book engine). Per message it applies, builds one ~433-byte UPDATE record carrying the *full* merged ticker state (static + trading state + top-10 book + trade summary + an order-level delta), writes it to `jnxdb` first (DB is authoritative), then multicasts the same bytes.
- **`jnxdb`** is a single-threaded queryable mirror — 5 tables keyed by ticker (static, state, order-level book, aggregated book, trades) plus an operator query port. It is also the FH's recovery source: a restarted `jnxfh` downloads the order store and last sequence from `jnxdb` and resumes the exchange session at `last_seq+1` — no start-of-day replay. A restarted `jnxdb` is simply re-synced from the FH's memory. No disk persistence anywhere, by design.
- **`jnxweb`** joins the multicast and serves a self-contained web page (hand-rolled HTTP + RFC 6455 WebSocket on one `selectors` loop) showing live book/trades per ticker. Because every record is full-state, packet loss self-heals — clients need no recovery protocol.
- The **wire format** is a frozen, versioned contract (`docs/wire_spec.md`), implemented twice (C++ and Python 3.6) and pinned by cross-language golden vectors generated by one side and field-asserted by the other.

**How correctness was enforced with cheap worker models** — three mechanical gates, not code review:
1. **Golden vectors** generated by the proven Phase-1 codec pinned every byte of the new C++ codecs (F1).
2. **The parity diff** (F2): canonical state dumps from the prototype and the C++ engine, `diff -r` empty over the full golden day — re-run after every later change to the market core.
3. **Scenario invariants** (F6): every crash/restart path must end in state identical to the uninterrupted baseline, with kills triggered by observed progress (SIGKILL, no sleeps). This gate caught a genuine bug in the resync dump (wrongly-keyed rows for tickers whose orders had all closed) — exactly the class of defect that survives unit tests.

**Environment discipline.** The dev container (Ubuntu, gcc 15, Python 3.14, no docker) is nothing like the target (RHEL 8.10, gcc 8.5, Python 3.6, no third-party packages). Countermeasures: `-std=c++11 -Wall -Wextra -Werror -pedantic` on every compile; a purpose-built AST linter (`tools/py36check.py`) gating all production Python; and a `dist` tarball proven to build with only gcc and make. Final assurance still requires the `TARGET_VALIDATION.md` checklist on a real target box.

**Deliverables inventory**: `cpp/` (≈ itch/soup/wire codecs, market core, db, fh, 65 tests, strict Makefile with `bench`/`test-asan`/`dist`), `jnxweb/`, `tools/` (dbquery, mcast_spy, ws_probe, compare_db_dump, run_e2e with 6 scenarios, py36check, dump/vector generators), docs (`wire_spec.md`, `OPERATIONS.md`, `RECOVERY.md`, `TARGET_VALIDATION.md`, sample configs), and the dist tarball.

## Observations on the AI-agent process

- The **plan-as-contract** approach worked: each phase prompt embedded the ground rules, the protocol cheat sheet, exact verification commands, and known pitfalls; Sonnet agents then rarely needed correction. The two intervention types that mattered were environmental (session-limit interruptions — resumed cleanly from transcripts) and architectural (deciding gates, freezing formats, folding discovered semantics back into the plan).
- **Independent re-verification before every commit** caught nothing the agents' own runs hadn't — but it is what made their green claims trustworthy, and it is cheap.
- Fresh agents with complete prompts beat long-lived agents once transcripts grew: F6–F8 each ran cold, faster and with no context tax.
- Cost profile: five Sonnet agents, ~1.5M subagent tokens total, over roughly two days of elapsed sessions for all of Phase 2.

## Known limitations / follow-ups

1. `jnxweb` imports the prototype's `jnxfeed.net.reactor` (the dist ships a shim) — should be made self-contained.
2. The simulator renumbers sequences from 1 (no offset option), so integration assertions are count-based rather than using original pcap sequence numbers.
3. Order-number collisions across order-book groups remain an open exchange question (Q1 in `CONNECTIVITY.md`); the code counts and warns.
4. The full-pipeline benchmark (~38k msg/s) is bounded by the Python simulator, not the C++ (decode+apply ceiling ~630k msg/s); a C++ feeder would be needed to measure the true pipeline limit.
5. Target-box validation and Japannext UAT are the only steps between this state and production readiness — see `TARGET_VALIDATION.md`.

# Japannext ITCH Feed Handler — Project Wrap-Up (Phases 1 & 2)

*2026-07-12 — repo `/workspace/jnx-feed`, plans `JNX_PLAN.md` (Phase 1) and `JNX_PLAN2.md` (Phase 2)*

## Executive summary

- **Goal**: evaluate AI-agent-driven development on a realistic market-data project — a Japannext PTS equities ITCH feed handler — built entirely from public specs, with no access to a real exchange line.
- **Phase 1 (Python prototype)**: delivered in full and **confirmed perfect in real use** — SoupBinTCP connectivity, complete ITCH v2.02 decoding, GLIMPSE snapshot recovery, order books/trades, CLI views, plus an offline exchange simulator. It connected to the exchange, recovered messages, and displayed live market data correctly.
- **Phase 2 (production-shaped C++ system)**: a three-process architecture — C++ feed handler (`jnxfh`), C++ in-memory database (`jnxdb`), Python 3.6 web GUI (`jnxweb`) — implemented in 9 gated phases (F0–F8), one commit per phase, **all complete**.
- **Correctness anchor**: the C++ market core is proven **bit-identical** to the trusted Python prototype over the full 222,189-message official sample — a diff of canonical state dumps that stayed empty through every later change.
- **Resilience proven live**: five kill/restart scenarios (FH crash, DB crash, exchange drop, snapshot cold-start, dual crash) all converge to state identical to an uninterrupted run; the testing itself flushed out and fixed one real recovery bug.
- **Performance**: C++ decode ~990k msg/s, decode+apply ~630k msg/s (vs 379k for the Python prototype's decode+apply); the full 222k-message trading day replays through the entire pipeline in seconds; RSS flat over a 10-minute soak; ASAN/UBSAN clean.
- **Constraints honored throughout**: C++11 only, Python 3.6 stdlib only, zero third-party dependencies (target: locked-down RHEL 8.10), all enforced mechanically (strict compiler flags, an AST-based py3.6 linter) because the dev container runs much newer toolchains.
- **Process**: all coding by Sonnet worker agents (≤2 concurrent) driven by detailed per-phase task prompts from the plan; every phase independently re-verified by the orchestrator before commit. Test count grew from 203 (Phase 1) to 65 C++ + 272 Python tests.
- **Remaining before go-live**: run `TARGET_VALIDATION.md` on a real RHEL 8.10 box, then UAT connectivity per `CONNECTIVITY.md` when Japannext access is granted.

## Phase 1 in brief (the foundation Phase 2 stood on)

Phase 1 (plan `JNX_PLAN.md`, tasks T1.1–T7.2) built a pure-Python 3.6, stdlib-only prototype: SoupBinTCP client with lossless reconnect-resume, declarative ITCH codec, GLIMPSE snapshot bootstrap, reference data / order-book / trade-tape stores, CLI views, and — critically — an **exchange simulator** and **golden fixtures** extracted from Japannext's official sample pcaps (222,189 messages, byte-verified against the spec). It was validated against the real exchange and worked as designed. Three Phase-1 artifacts became Phase 2's safety net: the proven data-handling logic (the porting reference), the simulator (the stand-in test line), and the golden sample (the regression oracle).

## Phase 2: what was built

**Architecture** — one exchange message becomes exactly one atomic unit of work end to end:

```
exchange/simulator ──SoupBinTCP──▶ jnxfh (C++) ──UDS records──▶ jnxdb (C++, 5 tables)
                                      │
                                      └──UDP multicast (full-state records)──▶ jnxweb (Py3.6) ──▶ browser
```

- **`jnxfh`** owns the live market state (the ported book engine). Per message it applies, builds one ~433-byte UPDATE record carrying the *full* merged ticker state (static + trading state + top-10 book + trade summary + an order-level delta), writes it to `jnxdb` first (DB is authoritative), then multicasts the same bytes.
- **`jnxdb`** is a single-threaded queryable mirror — 5 tables keyed by ticker (static, state, order-level book, aggregated book, trades) plus an operator query port. It is also the FH's recovery source: a restarted `jnxfh` downloads the order store and last sequence from `jnxdb` and resumes the exchange session at `last_seq+1` — no start-of-day replay. A restarted `jnxdb` is simply re-synced from the FH's memory. No disk persistence anywhere, by design.
- **`jnxweb`** joins the multicast and serves a self-contained web page (hand-rolled HTTP + RFC 6455 WebSocket on one `selectors` loop) showing live book/trades per ticker. Because every record is full-state, packet loss self-heals — clients need no recovery protocol.
- The **wire format** is a frozen, versioned contract (`docs/wire_spec.md`), implemented twice (C++ and Python 3.6) and pinned by cross-language golden vectors generated by one side and field-asserted by the other.

**How correctness was enforced with cheap worker models** — three mechanical gates, not code review:
1. **Golden vectors** generated by the proven Phase-1 codec pinned every byte of the new C++ codecs (F1).
2. **The parity diff** (F2): canonical state dumps from the prototype and the C++ engine, `diff -r` empty over the full golden day — re-run after every later change to the market core.
3. **Scenario invariants** (F6): every crash/restart path must end in state identical to the uninterrupted baseline, with kills triggered by observed progress (SIGKILL, no sleeps). This gate caught a genuine bug in the resync dump (wrongly-keyed rows for tickers whose orders had all closed) — exactly the class of defect that survives unit tests.

**Environment discipline.** The dev container (Ubuntu, gcc 15, Python 3.14, no docker) is nothing like the target (RHEL 8.10, gcc 8.5, Python 3.6, no third-party packages). Countermeasures: `-std=c++11 -Wall -Wextra -Werror -pedantic` on every compile; a purpose-built AST linter (`tools/py36check.py`) gating all production Python; and a `dist` tarball proven to build with only gcc and make. Final assurance still requires the `TARGET_VALIDATION.md` checklist on a real target box.

**Deliverables inventory**: `cpp/` (≈ itch/soup/wire codecs, market core, db, fh, 65 tests, strict Makefile with `bench`/`test-asan`/`dist`), `jnxweb/`, `tools/` (dbquery, mcast_spy, ws_probe, compare_db_dump, run_e2e with 6 scenarios, py36check, dump/vector generators), docs (`wire_spec.md`, `OPERATIONS.md`, `RECOVERY.md`, `TARGET_VALIDATION.md`, sample configs), and the dist tarball.

## Observations on the AI-agent process

- The **plan-as-contract** approach worked: each phase prompt embedded the ground rules, the protocol cheat sheet, exact verification commands, and known pitfalls; Sonnet agents then rarely needed correction. The two intervention types that mattered were environmental (session-limit interruptions — resumed cleanly from transcripts) and architectural (deciding gates, freezing formats, folding discovered semantics back into the plan).
- **Independent re-verification before every commit** caught nothing the agents' own runs hadn't — but it is what made their green claims trustworthy, and it is cheap.
- Fresh agents with complete prompts beat long-lived agents once transcripts grew: F6–F8 each ran cold, faster and with no context tax.
- Cost profile: five Sonnet agents, ~1.5M subagent tokens total, over roughly two days of elapsed sessions for all of Phase 2.

## Known limitations / follow-ups

1. `jnxweb` imports the prototype's `jnxfeed.net.reactor` (the dist ships a shim) — should be made self-contained.
2. The simulator renumbers sequences from 1 (no offset option), so integration assertions are count-based rather than using original pcap sequence numbers.
3. Order-number collisions across order-book groups remain an open exchange question (Q1 in `CONNECTIVITY.md`); the code counts and warns.
4. The full-pipeline benchmark (~38k msg/s) is bounded by the Python simulator, not the C++ (decode+apply ceiling ~630k msg/s); a C++ feeder would be needed to measure the true pipeline limit.
5. Target-box validation and Japannext UAT are the only steps between this state and production readiness — see `TARGET_VALIDATION.md`.

input.content:
# Phase 3 Proposal — Production Deployment & Multi-Exchange Generalization

*Status: PROPOSAL for discussion — not yet a task-level plan. 2026-07-12.*
*Prerequisites: Phase 2 complete (see `PROJECT_WRAPUP.md`); `TARGET_VALIDATION.md` run on a real RHEL 8.10 box; Japannext UAT passed per `CONNECTIVITY.md`.*

## 1. Goals

1. **Production readiness**: run the Japannext stack unattended in a real trading environment — supervised processes, redundancy, day-rollover, operational tooling good enough that support staff (not the authors) can run it.
2. **Observability & support**: first-class monitoring (metrics, health, latency), alerting, and in-production capture/replay for incident analysis.
3. **Generalization**: refactor the architecture so the next exchange (or the next protocol: MoldUDP64, FIX, proprietary binary multicast) is a bounded plug-in effort — reusing the DB, wire format, publication, recovery, web client, and ops tooling unchanged.

Non-goals for Phase 3: order entry / trading connectivity; historical tick database; UI beyond operator needs; performance work beyond what production data rates demand (Phase 2 headroom is ~15×).

## 2. Workstream A — Production hardening

**A1. Process supervision & packaging.** systemd units for jnxdb/jnxfh/jnxweb (Restart=always with backoff, dedicated non-root user, resource limits); RPM or versioned tarball + install script fitting the locked-down RHEL host; config under `/etc/jnx/`, logs to journald + rotated files; `--version` build stamping (git sha) in every binary and every log header.

**A2. Redundancy.**
- *Feed-handler pairs*: primary/standby `jnxfh` (standby logged in to the exchange but not publishing, or cold with fast DB-recovery start — decide with measured failover budgets). Arbitration via a small lease in `jnxdb` (it is already the shared state holder) rather than a new coordination service.
- *A/B exchange lines* (when Japannext provides them): consume both, first-wins arbitration keyed on sequence number — this drops out naturally from the transport generalization in Workstream C.
- *DB/client fan-out*: multiple `jnxdb`/`jnxweb` instances are already supported by multicast; document and test the multi-host topology (TTL, IGMP, network requirements) with the network team.

**A3. Session lifecycle & time.** Day rollover automation (scheduled end-of-session shutdown on ITCH `C`/Soup `Z`, pre-open fresh start, stale-state refusal if the DB holds yesterday's session); NTP/PTP sanity checks at startup; explicit handling of Japannext's day/night market groups (two concurrent sessions — currently untested territory; needs a decision: one process per group vs multi-session in one process).

**A4. Fault-injection hardening.** Extend the F6 scenario suite: malformed exchange data (fuzz the decoder with a corpus built from mutated golden fixtures), slow/black-holed multicast, DB slow-consumer (write timeout path), disk-full logging, clock jumps, SIGSTOP pauses (heartbeat expiry on both sides). Every scenario gets a runbook entry.

## 3. Workstream B — Monitoring & support

**B1. Metrics endpoint.** Both C++ processes expose their existing stats as a plain-text HTTP `/metrics` in Prometheus exposition format (hand-rolled — it is a trivial text format, no library needed; reuse the query-listener plumbing). Core series: msgs/s by type, exchange seq lag (recv time vs exchange `T`-clock), pub_seq, db_connected, reconnect counts, orphan/collision counters, mcast send errors, RSS, per-stage latency histograms (decode→apply→db-write→mcast, coarse buckets, measured with the existing `mono_ns`). `jnxweb` gets the same for received-gap statistics.
- Alert catalogue (doc + sample rules): feed silent > N s during market hours, seq lag beyond threshold, db_connected=false, restart storms, gap rate on any client.

**B2. Latency truth.** Timestamp every UPDATE with FH receive time (field exists as `exch_ns`; add `recv_ns` in wire spec **v2** — see C3) so any consumer can measure end-to-end freshness; a `tools/latency_probe.py` that reports percentiles from the live multicast.

**B3. Production capture & replay.** An always-on capture (the Phase-1 `.itch` format, rotated hourly) written by `jnxfh` — this is both the incident-analysis record and future regression fixtures from *real* data. `run_e2e.py` learns to replay a captured hour against a candidate build and diff final state vs the production DB dump (the F2/F6 machinery reused for release validation).

**B4. Support tooling.** `jnxctl` — one operator CLI wrapping today's scattered tools (dbquery, mcast_spy, health, failover trigger, capture management); admin commands on the query port (log-level at runtime, connection status, forced resync); an on-call one-pager per alert linking into `RECOVERY.md`.

## 4. Workstream C — Generalization to multiple exchanges & protocols

The Phase 2 design already has the right seams; Phase 3 makes them explicit interfaces.

**C1. Layering target.**

```
[Transport]      SoupBinTCP | MoldUDP64+rewinder | FIX session | raw mcast | file/pcap replay
      ▼ ordered (seq, payload) stream + gap/recovery semantics per transport
[Decoder]        JNX ITCH | <exchange X> ITCH-dialect | FIX app messages | ...
      ▼ normalized book events: AddOrder/Exec/Delete/Replace/State/RefData/Trade
[Market core]    ONE shared engine (today's cpp/market) + per-exchange policy hooks
      ▼ ApplyResult
[Publication]    ONE record builder / wire format / jnxdb / multicast / jnxweb / tools
```

- *Transport interface*: `start / stop / on_payload(seq, bytes) / request_from(seq)`; each implementation owns its gap semantics (Soup = resume-on-reconnect; MoldUDP64 = A/B arbitration + re-request/rewinder; FIX = ResendRequest). Phase 1's Mold unwrapping code and pcaps give MoldUDP64 a ready-made test bed.
- *Normalized event model*: the key design decision. Today `Market::apply` takes JNX ITCH structs; define an exchange-neutral event set (superset fields, price scale + sentinel conventions carried in instrument metadata) and make the JNX decoder emit it. The F2 parity gate guarantees the refactor is behavior-neutral: the golden diff must stay empty.
- *Policy hooks* for the per-exchange quirks Phase 2 hard-coded (ref-price-in-`A`, passive-price trades, order-number scoping, absence semantics) — a small strategy interface, JNX as the first implementation.

**C2. FIX support (scoped).** For market data, FIX means a session layer (Logon/Heartbeat/TestRequest/ResendRequest/SequenceReset over TCP, tag=value parsing — hand-rolled, stdlib-only is feasible and keeps the zero-dependency rule) plus MarketDataSnapshot/IncrementalRefresh (35=W/X) decoding to the normalized events. Deliverable: transport + decoder + a FIX flavor of the simulator; book semantics reuse the shared core. FAST encoding, if a target exchange needs it, is a separate later work item.

**C3. Multi-exchange data model.** Wire spec **v2**: add `exchange` id + `recv_ns` to the envelope, version-bump with dual-decode support in clients during migration (the version byte and the frozen-vector test rig make this safe). `jnxdb` tables keyed by (exchange, ticker, group); `jnxweb` grows an exchange selector; instrument metadata (price scale, lot, tick tables) becomes per-exchange configuration rather than JNX constants.

**C4. Proof-of-generality milestone.** The workstream is DONE when a second feed runs through the shared stack with *only* transport+decoder+policy code being new. Candidates, in order of increasing effort: (1) JNX **MoldUDP64** service — same messages, new transport, official pcap already in hand, exercises A/B arbitration = the redundancy feature A2 needs anyway; (2) another Nasdaq-style ITCH exchange; (3) a FIX-based feed. Recommendation: **do (1) as the Phase-3 milestone**, keep (2)/(3) as fast-follow.

## 5. Suggested sequencing & sizing

| Stage | Content | Depends on |
|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | Target-box validation + UAT (carry-over) | exchange access |
| P3.1 | A1 packaging/systemd + B1 metrics + B4 jnxctl skeleton | — |
| P3.2 | B2 latency + B3 capture/replay + A4 fault-injection round | P3.1 |
| P3.3 | C1 layering refactor behind the parity gate (no behavior change) | P3.1 |
| P3.4 | MoldUDP64 transport + A/B arbitration (= C4 milestone + A2 line redundancy) | P3.3 |
| P3.5 | A2 FH failover pair + A3 rollover automation | P3.2, P3.4 |
| P3.6 | C2 FIX transport/decoder + C3 wire v2 multi-exchange model | P3.3 |

Rough effort in Phase-2 currency: P3.1–P3.2 ≈ one Phase-2 phase each; P3.3–P3.4 ≈ the F2+F5 pair; P3.6 is the largest single item (FIX session testing discipline). The same agent-worker method applies — each stage gets plan-grade prompts, mechanical gates (parity diff, scenario invariants, golden vectors extended per protocol), and real-data capture from B3 as new regression fixtures.

## 6. Open questions for the user / exchange

1. Failover budget: acceptable data-unavailability window on FH failure? (Drives hot-standby vs cold-restart in A2.)
2. Day/night groups: do we need both concurrently in production, and one process or two?
3. Monitoring stack on site: is Prometheus/Grafana available in the locked environment, or do we alert from logs alone?
4. Which second exchange/protocol is actually next commercially? (Reorders C4's candidates.)
5. Does the "no third-party libraries" rule hold for Phase 3 production too (it shaped B1's hand-rolled exporter and C2's hand-rolled FIX)?
6. Multicast in production: routed (needs IGMP/PIM coordination) or single-segment?
